data.title: Webull: We See 60% Downside From Here
data.description:
A few weeks ago, conservative media company Newsmax (NMAX) listed on the public markets to much fanfare.

With an initial trading price of roughly $14 per share, the stock went on to rally more than 1,800% following a massive frenzy from investors. Briefly, the company was more valuable than Fox Corporation (FOX), another conservative media firm many consider to be NMAX's much, much larger rival.

Following this initial excitement, however, shares have languished, trading back down into the $20-per-share range. This represents a massive wipeout of more than 90% - from highs to lows - for investors, driving losses for almost anyone who bought into the spike:

To many market watchers, this cycle was somewhat predictable, given NMAX's breakeven profitability, small operating footprint, and ludicrous valuation.

Monday, shares of Webull (NASDAQ:BULL) rallied more than 370% in one of the hottest new issue debuts of the last few years - similar to NMAX (Although the stock has since come off somewhat).

The excitement here is being driven by the considerable interest that institutional investors have in Fintech companies, but also the firm's tightly held share structure. Sure, there are a lot of shares outstanding, but the founders and insiders hold a great deal of this float. Thus, the circulating share count may actually be smaller than it appears on paper, which could be behind the stock price's recent elasticity. Less shares means that smaller amounts of money can push the price around more quickly.

That may be what we're seeing here.

Now, at this price point, BULL is valued at roughly $17+ billion dollars. To us, this price is simply ridiculous, given that the company only did ~$390 million in revenue in each of the last three calendar years. This places the sales multiple at roughly 43x.

Over the long run, we see value in Webull. The company's operational footprint is robust, the product offering is solid, and we see the potential for material growth in top and bottom-line results in the coming years.

However, for a company that hasn't grown revenues as of late, this price point appears way out of whack. Thus, we're expecting a similar, NMAX-style price drop over the following year or so.

Today, we'll outline BULL's financial profile, talk about the stock's valuation, and explain why we see considerable downside in the company's shares.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

BULL's Valuation

Normally, we'd talk first about the company's financials, but there's a bit of a unique situation going on with Webull.

Given that the stock just completed a SPAC in the last few days, there's very little 'hard' financial information about the company out there right now. Even figuring out the basics like share count and valuation has required an extensive search.

As we've been able to piece together from regulatory filings and other financial data, the company has roughly ~485 million shares outstanding at the present moment.

In the company's recent F-4 filing, the below table outlines how many shares could potentially be outstanding following a business combination:

As you can see, most of the new listed company is owned by former BULL investors, although some of the SPAC investors are also in on the action.

Depending on how many shares were redeemed in the transaction (a number we haven't been able to find), this table outlines that there may be anywhere between 515 million and 490 million shares outstanding. This is in slight contrast with market cap data we're seeing on the company's website and in a recent CNBC article, which indicate an outstanding share base between 460 million shares and 470 million shares. All told, it's a bit of a mess when it comes to the data, but we'll be operating under the assumption that BULL has roughly ~485 million shares outstanding, which is essentially the midpoint from all of the information that's out there.

With a stock price on Wednesday of $35.37, this gives the company a market cap in the ~$17-billion-dollar range.

This makes BULL larger than peers like SoFi (SOFI), and in the same ballpark (tens of billions in market cap) as industry heavyweights like Robinhood (HOOD):

Company Market Cap Sales Multiple
Schwab $139 billion 7.2x
Interactive Brokers $73 billion 3.6x
Robinhood $39 billion 13x
Webull $17 billion 43x
SoFi $12 billion 4.3x

Unfortunately, there aren't that many great public comps for BULL, as other firms like EToro, Public, and TastyTrade remain privately held.

That said, we'd argue that the company's stock, at this price, appears expensive. The current market cap already positions the stock as the 6th largest brokerage company listed in the United States.

We'll talk more about this later.

Now that we have some idea of where BULL is currently positioned from a size and valuation perspective, let's take a look at some of the company's underlying financials.

BULL's Financials

As we mentioned, many of BULL's 'hard' financial performance metrics aren't available, as the firm hasn't yet released public, audited numbers in the form of a 10Q or 10K.

Right now, we're going off of BULL's investor presentation, which highlights a couple of things.

First off, on the product side, we're actually quite happy with BULL as an operator within the space.

Over the last few years, the company has built up a huge account base (more than 23 million users), alongside a robust suite of offerings.

Catering to both casual investors and more active traders, BULL offers access to a number of investment products and features across a relatively wide number of global markets:

Since 2022, the company has expanded regulatory licenses from 3 to 12, expanding the TAM and growth potential of the company. Not only that, but BULL's actual offering includes basically everything you'd ever want (aside from crypto), for both traders and investors:

When viewed in a more structured layout, we'd argue that the breadth and competitiveness (zero commissions) of the offering is robust, even when compared with other firms like HOOD:

Assets Accounts Tools Platforms

Stocks & ETFs

 Cash Recurring Investments Web
Options Margin Smart Advisor Desktop
Index Options IRA Fractional Trading Mobile
Futures Overnight Trading
Bonds Charting, Screeners, Research
Money Market Funds

That said, the company's financials don't necessarily reflect this reality.

Nominal revenues have been solid, but ongoing expansions have dampened recent profitability, highlighting the depth of recent investments, and how long it might take for those investments to pay off.

For revenues, BULL's top line has largely remained flat from 2022 through today:

While the company may highlight that this performance as 'self-hedging', and thus, a good thing, the lack of growth - in a period where other companies have grown substantially - is somewhat concerning.

Similarly, on the bottom line, tightening margins have characterized recent years' performance, as stronger investments into marketing and G&A have driven down adjusted operating income:

This dynamic is typically one you'd see during an expansion, so it isn't problematic in and of itself, but when paired with the static revenues, it's something to think harder about.

All in all, what little we have in the way of BULL's financial performance shows that the company has a clear product-market fit with respect to the client experience, although monetizing those customers has been a challenge.

Plus, the company's multi-country strategy means that the firm's 23.3 million+ client base could be worth significantly less in LTV than HOOD's almost fully American user cohort, by comparison.

Should You Buy Or Sell Webull?

So - this brings us to the million-dollar question. Should you buy Webull? Or sell it, given the generous stock price?

To answer that, we'll need to make a couple of assumptions. First off, we're anticipating that as brokerage headwinds dissipate and investments begin to pay an ROI, that BULL's top line results will begin growing once more.

Not only that, but we're also anticipating that margins will re-expand off the back of this growth. Neither of these dynamics may play out, but its' how we see the future going, given the offering quality. With product breadth and depth like BULL, there's always an opportunity for growth.

From there, we see BULL's revenues hitting the $500 million mark by the end of 2026. This is a rough prediction, but its' based, again, on the company getting ROI from the expanding global market presence.

Finally, on the valuation front, as we comped out earlier, BULL - in that growth case - could be worth anywhere between 6x and 10x sales. SOFI isn't a great comparison at the lower end of the range, but BULL's results are dwarfed by HOOD's stronger NA entrenchment and more deliberate foreign offerings. Thus, multiples between 13x and 4x.

At 6x - 10x the potential of FY'26 $500 million in sales, you're looking at a market cap of roughly $3 - $5 billion. This is a significant discount vs. where the market is currently trading, which is at a much higher ~$17 billion valuation. Thus, we see significant 60%+ downside from the current stock price, which results in our 'Strong Sell' rating.

For reference, this indicates a price in the $10 - $11 per share range.

Risks

On the 'risk' side of things, we've covered both the positive and negative aspects of the company.

However, when taking something like this short, it's worth considering that frenzies and rallies can last for several days or weeks, and that a 'short' at this price may be nowhere near the top.

Thus, we'd argue that the real way to manage risk here, if one was to take it 'short', would be by sizing appropriately.

Even if you're not looking to take it short, if you're currently long, then we'd advocate that you sell out at the current price. No need to be greedy with an already monstrous gain.

Summary

Taken together, here's our overarching view: the company has a solid offering, and the business is stable. Plus, there's opportunity for more growth in the coming quarters from the expanding TAM and feature set quality.

However, at this price, the market has baked in way too much growth and excitement. As a result, even with the potentially strong underlying performance, we're finding it hard to rate BULL as anything other than a 'Strong Sell' for the coming year.

Stay safe out there!

data.title: TSMC Has Finally Become Dirt Cheap Following Its Q1 Results (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I initiated my investment coverage of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) here on Seeking Alpha in late February 2024 with a "Buy" rating, which hasn't aged well so far, given the recent volatility spike on the markets and rising concerns around the US-China trade war with all subsequent consequences.

My Updated Thesis

In my most recent article, which was a bullish update, I argued that TSMC was undervalued by over 22% given its marginality levels and also the news about OpenAI's partnership. Now, even when I account for the rising risks and uncertainty in valuation calculations, my valuation findings say that TSM stock has simply become too undervalued to ignore. The firm's recent news - specifically the rise in pricing for its chips - suggests that TSMC has an unbeatable moat in its niche with a solid pricing power, which should compensate well for most tariffs and possible growth limitations. I'm upgrading TSM stock to "Strong Buy" following its fantastic Q1 FY2025 print.

TSM's Q1 FY2025 Review

As I'm writing these lines, Taiwan Semiconductor managed to release its Q1 FY2025 results just a couple of hours ago, beating the consensus estimates, showing a remarkable consistency across its business units:

With consolidated revenue of ~$25.53 billion (+35.3% YoY) and diluted EPS of ~$2.12 (per ADR unit, +60% YoY), the slight sequential dip of 3.4% (5.1% in USD terms) compared to Q4 2024 looks much better across the main segments compared to what could be observed in 2024/2023. We see some powerful underlying demand drivers, particularly the continued ramp-up in AI-related applications, which partially offset the smartphone softness, based on the management's commentary.

But what impresses me most are the sustained, industry-leading margins: TSM's GP margin came in at a stellar 58.8%, while operating and net margins were at 48.5% and 43.1%, respectively. The firm's pricing power is evident, especially so in its most advanced nodes. TSMC isn't just an ordinary chip manufacturer; it is the indispensable foundry partner for nearly every major fabless semiconductor company globally, being the world's largest dedicated chip foundry, with ~65% market share, according to Statista. The global semiconductor foundry market should reach ~$321.1 billion by 2034 in TAM, according to Global Markets Insights (with a CAGR of >9%), and TSM's wide economic moat there is what its peers struggle to breach. So I believe the market's recent pessimism has excessively discounted the company's fundamental strengths and long-term growth trajectory, given the estimated CAGR of the end market.

Free cash flow in Q1 also saw a healthy increase, rising by $1.12 billion to $9.07 billion (my conversion here is based on today's FX, as is), even as the company invested heavily with CAPEX totaling ~$10.06 billion during the quarter. At the same time, the amount of cash on the balance sheet went up by 37.9% YoY in (NT$ currency), with shareholders' equity rising by 64.5% YoY; the ROE of 32.7% showed an YoY expansion of 730 bps, which is a lot.

Looking ahead, the management guided for Q2 FY2025 revenue to be between $28.4-29.2 billion, way above the consensus estimate of ~$27.22 billion prevailing before the announcement (based on SA News), which is a great sequential growth path (+12.8% QoQ at the midpoint):

TSMC also expects to maintain its exceptional profitability with a GP margin of 58% and an EBIT margin of 48% (both at the midpoint) despite the swirling tariff uncertainties.

So, assuming the management could overlook the world's development since Trump's inauguration, it's clear to me that they still see a strong demand for their cutting-edge 3nm and 5nm technologies, which power the most demanding applications in AI, high-performance computing, and premium smartphones.

The Recent Developments And Tariffs

Tariffs are now the elephant in the room for any semiconductor company, and the problem here is that the news flow surrounding this has been fast, furious, and, to be honest, a bit confusing. Initially, as you might remember, President Trump announced "reciprocal tariffs," including rates cited as 34% for China and 32% for Taiwan; and even as the fear of such tariffs disrupting supply chains, inflating costs for consumers and businesses, and potentially dampening demand for electronics manufactured using TSMC's chips arose, Trump added fuel to the fire stating that "he warned TSMC it would face a hefty tax (up to 100%) if it didn't build factories in the U.S.":

I can't know what exactly influenced Trump then, but the administration subsequently announced exemptions for a range of popular electronic goods - smartphones, laptops, computers, processors, and memory chips - primarily imported from China. This naturally gave a massive relief, causing a rebound in many tech stocks. But then again, the Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick soon characterized the exemptions as merely a "temporary reprieve," and the tech sector fell off a cliff.

While politicians "are trading" during this ongoing trade war, I think what's important amid all that are signals coming from tech companies, which were considered "wide-moaty". TSMC is exactly such a company, and even if we don't look at its just-released Q1 figures (those may not be relevant in the next few quarters), we can see from the recent announcements from the firm that its pricing power is still there:

According to a report by the Economic Daily News, market sources suggest that TSMC is considering raising prices for 4nm chip production at its U.S. facility by as much as 30%, citing robust demand and rising costs.

It looks like all the tariffs risk for TSMC has already been priced in, perhaps excessively so, given the exemptions (however temporary) and the sheer indispensability of TSMC's services. I mean, disrupting TSMC would mean disrupting the entire global tech ecosystem, an outcome even a tariff-heavy administration might hesitate to fully trigger. And while everyone is scared, the news of rising prices should silently lead to even higher margins for TSM (or at least to their longer-term stabilization).

TSM's Valuation Update

Right away, the market's consensus sees TSM's EPS for FY2025 at $9.07, which is 28.89% above last year's figure. The implied price-to-earnings of the firm (if it reports in line with the estimate) is ~16.71x. So the FY2025 PEG ratio is currently ~0.58x. Many investment banks - Bank of America analysts, for example, lowered their EPS estimates recently, but kept their price targets above the current stock price. BofA analysts, while lowering their price target slightly "due to tariff concerns and earthquake impacts", reiterated their "Buy" rating, saying the sell-off was "likely overdone" and noting that the stock's valuation "appeared attractive at 14x 2025E P/E compared to a trough of 10x during the 2022 downturn".

So I assume that the demand side is still strong enough to offset any concerning actions of TSM's partners (I mean, their potential decrease in orders). While TSMC has been beating the consensus figures for the past years since 2018, I'll assume the current EPS estimate for FY2025 is correct. So what's the fair P/E multiple for TSM, then? Well, given a likely multiple contraction, I think it's about 22x, given the historical norms and trends:

This gives me a fair value per share of ~$200, which is about 32% above the current stock price. This makes TSM a real bargain, even considering quite pessimistic inputs and no earnings beating in the next few quarters.

Risks And Your Takeaway

The main risk for TSM right now, like for almost any other tech stock, remains the geopolitical situation. A possible escalation of tensions between China and Taiwan or the actual introduction of highly disruptive US tariffs would drive down TSM stock price well below the current levels, no matter how strong its financials looked in the recent past. Also, the semiconductor industry is really inherently cyclical, we have to keep this in mind. Although the rise of AI seems to be creating strong secular growth tailwinds for TSM and its peers, I see an execution risk for TSMC's massive global expansion projects (Arizona, Japan, Germany) - in case the demand is satisfied and the pricing power of TSM is lost somehow, the current/prospective projects' ROI will unlikely to sufficient. If so, the valuation multiple might drop further.

Despite all these risk factors, I think that TSMC has a unique place in the semi tech sector. Its wide moat in a niche so crucial for the global AI usage expansion, it really can't be overestimated as of today, in my opinion. The recent developments, when we zoom out the tariffs fears and look at the demand/supply balance, strongly suggest that TSM's positioning is only getting stronger. So even if we use skeptical assumptions in our EPS and implied P/E ratio projections, we'll likely get a massive undervaluation. For what I took as inputs, my modeling says TSM stock is at least 32% undervalued today following the Q1 results (and after the dip in the stock price). So that's why I'm upgrading my rating to "Strong Buy" today.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: My Dividend Stock Portfolio: New All-Time Dividend Record In March - 100 Holdings With 15 Buys
data.description:
Portfolio Changes in March

In March 2025, U.S. markets took a hit as trade tensions flared up again and haven't stopped ever since. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq both dropped over 10% from recent highs, officially entering correction territory. The sell-off was sharp and triggered by a wave of new tariffs announced by President Trump in early April on imports from Canada, Mexico, and China, sparking fears of a renewed trade war.​

Tech stocks were hit hardest, while banks posted solid profits but warned that inflation and weaker consumer spending could be on the horizon if tariffs stay in place. Luxury brands had mixed results—some held up well, others not so much.

Markets ended the month on a cautious note, with eyes now on how trade talks unfold and what the Fed might do next. April kicked off with chaos after Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariffs sent markets into a tailspin. China hit back hard, the Dow plunged over 1,600 points, and protests erupted. Even with a partial pause, tariffs stayed at record highs, fueling recession fears.

My investment activity in March managed to reclaim the $1,000 threshold after last month's sharp drop. I channeled around $1,200 into the markets and plan to increase that further in April, given highly volatile markets with plenty of buying opportunities.

My main picks in March were Hercules Capital (HTGC), Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) and Ares Capital (ARCC).

In March, all my moves added $101. This is fractionally above my aim to lift my dividend income by about $100 each month, mostly doable thanks to the high yields from BDCs. I am hoping to increase that in the coming months to at least $120 and possibly $150.

Overall, the average yield on cost of my new investments in March averaged around 7.1%, reinforcing my belief in BDCs as a core part of my portfolio. I'm banking on their dividends staying steady - that's what I'm all about when it comes to generating income. Time will tell if I'm on the money or just overly optimistic.

All these purchases break down as follows:

All net purchases in March can be found below:

Dividend Income in March 2025

Dividend income hit a new all-time record of $1,404 in March, surpassing my expectations as I underestimated how much dividend income my purchases over the last twelve months actually contributed. This represents growth of 17% Y/Y and 21% sequentially. Adjusting for semi-annual dividend payments from Commonwealth Bank of Australia (OTCPK:CBAUF), Hercules Capital's odd dividend schedule and other smaller dividend payments returns Y/Y growth of 17% and 5% sequentially.

Beyond that, on an annual basis, the biggest income drivers are by far Ares Capital Corporation, a stock I am running at minimum on a bi-weekly savings plan (but most of the time I am buying stock at least once per week and sometimes daily) and where my contributions quickly translated into meaningful dividend income and the MidCap Financial Investment Corporation (MFIC). Dividends from Ares have already comfortably cleared the big psychological milestone of $200. This quarter's dividend amounts to $244 and will soon eclipse $250. Dividend income from MFIC is also rising quickly - although not on the same level as that from ARCC - and has now passed $125. Dividend income from Hercules Capital is on a roll and also cleared a psychological milestone of $150 coming in at almost $160 already.

Similarly striking is that net dividends from monthly dividend payers have again comfortably cleared the $100 level thanks to the big supplemental dividend from Main Street Capital.

In alignment with my investment strategy, I've incorporated a BDC/Non-BDC breakdown into my dividend income charts. This addition allows for more precise monitoring of this critical development.

Additionally, I have created a new visualization that dissects dividend income into two distinct categories: BDCs and Non-BDCs. These categories in turn are separated by dividend payment frequency into Monthly (blue) and Quarterly (orange) in absolute and relative terms.

For Q1 2025, BDCs accounted for 32% of my dividend income, up from 26% a year ago and down from the record 35% of Q4/2024.

This metric provides valuable insights and will help guide my investment decisions as it evolves over time.

In March, dividend income from my top three regular payers reached a staggering $445, a big jump from last year's average of $390 and getting closer to the major $500 level. I am buying MAIN on a monthly basis via savings plans and MidCap and Ares usually at least once a week. Based on that, I might be able to hit that $500 target in Q4 assuming at least stable dividends for the time being.

All this is depicted below:

Here is a chart that shows the development of my net dividend income by month over time between 2015 and 2025. You can see the growth of my dividend income and the average annual dividend for each year:

This chart is my favorite because it illustrates the progression of my dividend income over time and allows me to easily see the average annual dividend for each year.

Next, I have plotted all the individual dividend payments I have received, coloring them by year and arranging the years side by side instead of horizontally as in previous updates. This visualization allows for a more comprehensive analysis of the distribution of my dividend payments over time.

By visualizing the data in this manner, we get a clear picture of how dividend payments are spread out over different years, offering valuable insights into the overall growth of my dividend income over the years.

This visualization is initially quite cluttered, but it contains a wealth of information. It shows every individual dividend payment I have received since I began my investment journey in 2015, represented as a circle that is colored and sized according to its contribution. The view is organized by month and year, allowing for a more comprehensive analysis of the development of my dividend income over time. For each year and month, a white rectangle indicates the average monthly dividend. The area where dividends fall below the average is filled in dark red, while the area above the average is colored dark green.

This type of data visualization allows for a detailed examination of the distribution of my dividend payments over time and enables me to easily identify trends and patterns in my income growth.

Now, zooming in on March, we can immediately see a number of big green circles in a sea of black. The bigger the circle and the bigger the distance to the previous circle for the same stock, the bigger is the change in dividend income compared to the prior years.

The importance of Ares Capital Corporation is clear, with a reddish circle indicating the massive jump in dividend income over the last 12 months due to heavy ongoing monthly investments, and I have no plans to stop these monthly savings plans. Similarly, the big year-over-year increases in dividend income from MAIN, MFIC and GAIN also clearly stand out visually in that chart.

Apart from that, when it comes to the dividends for 2025, I notice mostly dark blue circles at the top of the scale, which is exactly what I want to see. These big circles represent rising dividend income, and my goal is to have as many of them as possible at the top of the chart.

Overall, my aim is to observe numerous large orange circles positioned at the highest point on the scale. This signifies a positive trend of increasing dividend income, which is precisely what I'm striving for.

Another way to express the monthly dividend income is in terms of Gifted Working Time (GWT). Assuming an average hourly rate of $37.34 for 2025, my GWT and annual net dividends have been as follows:

  • 2018: 121 hours GWT, $3,000 in annual net dividends
  • 2019: 142 hours GWT, $3,600 in annual net dividends
  • 2020: 152 hours GWT, $3,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2021: 180 hours GWT, $5,050 in annual net dividends
  • 2022: 229 hours GWT, $6,400 in annual net dividends
  • 2023: 272 hours GWT, $8,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2024: 284 hours GWT, $10,200 in annual net dividends
  • 2025: Targeting at least 295 hours GWT, $11,000 in annual net dividends

The overall target for the year with $11,000 in annual net dividends is very conservative, but I expect that I will have to take out some risk from my portfolio as I am eyeing to raise capital for real estate investments if I encounter the right deal and definitely for a lot of traveling. This would represent a growth rate of around 8% which admittedly is a low target but definitely something I am willing to update as the year unfolds. If things go differently, the dream target would be to pass the next milestone of $12,000 which would require around 18% dividend growth.

That would be a great achievement, but nothing I am banking on to reach this year - it would be the icing on the cake.

Across the years, my YTD Dividend Race has been as follows:

  • 2018: Disappointing
  • 2019: Phenomenal, benefiting from a low baseline in the prior year
  • 2020: Fairly disappointing
  • 2021: Phenomenal
  • 2022: Much better than expected, fueled by the very weak euro
  • 2023: Breathtaking
  • 2024: Simply great

I have updated the YTD Dividend Race chart to show the development of YTD dividend income on a daily basis, allowing us to see that most of my dividend income is generated mid-month and towards the end of the month. The chart also breaks down the development by month for the current year, making it easy to identify significant jumps in income.

Expressed in GWT, it presents itself as follows:

What this shows is as follows:

  1. All time (blue area) - Around 1,565 hours, or 196 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income since the start of my dividend journey. Assuming a five-day workweek, this translates into more than half a year in total.
  2. Full-year 2025 (green bars) - Around 73 hours, or 9.1 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income in 2025, which is basically equivalent to almost two full working weeks funded with dividends. That is great progress, and I can't wait to add another month to that statistic.
  3. Highlighted in pink is the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month (March).

This visualization allows us to see the overall impact of my dividend income on the amount of active work I am able to replace with passive income. It also enables us to see the progress made year-to-date, as well as the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month.

Upcoming April Dividends

As every month, April is packed with dividend payments heavily concentrated around the 15th and at month-end when the dividend checks from Canada's big banks as well as JPMorgan will be arriving over a period of just 3–4 days.

Given the majority of my savings plans feature companies that pay dividends in January such as W. P. Carey (WPC), Altria (MO), Cisco Systems (CSCO), JPMorgan, Toronto-Dominion Bank, Bank of Nova Scotia, Stryker (SYK), Medtronic (MDT) as well as ongoing investments into stocks like Rithm Capital (RITM) and Annaly Capital (NLY), it surely is going to be a big month that should easily set a new all-time dividend record for the month of April.

Overall, I am expecting more than $700 in dividend income and can't wait to see how the numbers will stack up in around 3 weeks.
